title: Why Most People Who Think They're Early Are Already Late

source.original: If you want to be early to a trend, you have to go really deep and be an expert – Parker

body_html:
There's a pervasive illusion in investing and trend-spotting: the belief that awareness equals advantage. Someone reads a viral thread, catches a podcast mention, or sees a ticker trending on social media and thinks they've discovered something before the crowd. But if a trend is legible enough to show up in mainstream channels, the earliest movers have likely already positioned themselves months or even years prior.

The uncomfortable truth is that true early positioning requires expertise so deep that you can identify inflection points before narratives form. This isn't about being plugged into the right Discord or following the right accounts. It's about having spent enough time in a specific domain that you recognize when something fundamental has shifted—before anyone has written the explainer article about it.

This dynamic creates a harsh filter. The people who are genuinely early to crypto infrastructure, AI model architectures, biotech breakthroughs, or energy transitions aren't generalists scanning headlines. They're specialists who have been immersed in the technical, regulatory, or market details for years. Their edge isn't speed—it's recognition.

For everyone else, the signal to noise ratio is brutal. By the time a trend is packaged into a compelling narrative that a broad audience can understand, the asymmetric upside has often been captured. What remains is a momentum trade dressed up as a conviction bet, held by people who lack the domain knowledge to distinguish between a temporary pullback and a thesis-breaking development.

The practical takeaway isn't to never invest in areas outside your expertise—it's to be brutally honest about where you stand on the awareness curve. If you can't articulate the technical or structural reasons a trend is emerging without referencing someone else's thesis, you're probably not early. You're audience.
